zh-hans.Gower, Glamorgan, Wales: 威尔士格拉莫爾根高維爾

nameMeanings.name: Ivor
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English: (i) relationship name from Old Scandinavian Ívarr, Iwar, Ivar ‘yew-army’. (ii) relationship name, possibly from the Welsh male given name Ifor; if so, it is not clear that it survives today.

variants.name: Ivers
meanings: Irish: from Ó hÍomhair, ‘descendant of Íomhar’, a Gaelic form of the Old Norse personal name Ívarr (see Iverson 2), with excrescent English -s. It is occasionally Anglicized as Howard . Also a shortened form of Mac Íomhair ‘son of Íomhar’, with excrescent English -s. See McIver .
North German: patronymic from the personal name Ivar (see Iversen ).

English: variant of Iver, with post-medieval excrescent -s, a habitational name from Iver (Buckinghamshire) named from Old English ȳfre ‘edge or brow of a hill, tip of a promontory’, or from a minor place called with the same word.

variants.name: Iver
meanings: 1 English: locative name from Iver (Bucks) named from Old English * ȳfre ‘edge or brow of a hill, tip of a promontory’, or from a minor place named with the same word.
2 Scottish: (i) relationship name, reduced form of Irish and Scottish Gaelic Mac Íomhair, see McIvor . (ii) relationship name from the Old Scandinavian personal name Ivar; see Ivor .

variants.name: Iverson
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Iver, Erik, Hjalmer, Lars, Selmer, Thor, Evald, Gunner, Helmer, Ingman, Jarl, Joneen.
Americanized form of Norwegian, Danish, and North German Iversen and of Swedish Ivarsson (see Ivarson ), all cognates of 2 below.

English (Kent and London): patronymic from Old Norse Ívarr, Iwar, Ivar, a compound of either ív ‘yew tree, bow’ or Ing (the name of a god) + ar ‘warrior’ or ‘spear’. Alternatively, the name might be a patronymic from the Welsh male personal name Ifor, itself from British Celtic Iūdomāros ‘lord’ + ‘great’, but if so, it is not clear that it survives today.

nameMeanings.name: Ynyr
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Traditional: pronounced ‘inn-eer’, probably from the Latin name Honorius (a derivative of honor ‘renown’).

nameMeanings.name: ap
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of any of the names beginning with this syllable.

variants.name: Alexander
meanings: From the Latin form of the Greek name Alexandros, from alexein ‘to defend’ + anēr ‘man, warrior’ (genitive andros). The name became extremely popular in the post-classical period, and was borne by several individuals in the New Testament and some early Christian saints. Its use as a common given name throughout Europe, however, derives largely from the fame of Alexander the Great , King of Macedon ( 356–323 bc ), around whom a large body of popular legend grew up in late antiquity, much of which came to be embodied in the medieval ‘Alexander romances’.

variants.name: Alan
meanings: Of Celtic origin and uncertain derivation (possibly a diminutive of a word meaning ‘rock’). It was introduced into England by Breton followers of William the Conqueror , most notably Alan , Earl of Brittany, who was rewarded for his services with vast estates in the newly conquered kingdom. In Britain the variants Allan and Allen are considerably less frequent, and generally represent transferred uses of surname forms, whereas in America all three forms of the name are approximately equally common. See also Alun .

variants.name: Albert
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

en.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor was born about 1208, in Gower, Glamorgan, Wales. She had at least 1 son with Ynyr ap Ivor.

fr.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor est née environ 1208, à Gower, Glamorgan, Galles. Elle a eu au moins 1 fils avec Ynyr ap Ivor.

de.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor wurde um 1208 in Gower, Glamorgan, Wales geboren. Sie hatte mit Ynyr ap Ivor mindestens 1 Sohn.

it.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor è nata il circa 1208 nel Gower, Glamorgan, Galles. Lei avuto almeno 1 figlio con Ynyr ap Ivor.

ja.Gower, Glamorgan, Wales: ウェールズグラモーガンゴウエル

ko.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor(은)는 대략 1208년 웨일스 글램르건 가워에서 태어났다. 그녀는 Ynyr ap Ivor(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들을 두었다.

pt.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor nasceu aproximadamente 1208, em Gower, Glamorgan, Gales. Ela teve pelo menos 1 filho com Ynyr ap Ivor.

ru.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor родилась около 1208 г. в местности гоуир, глэморган, Уэльс. У неё был по крайней мере 1 сын от Ynyr ap Ivor.

es.lifeSummary: Mrs. Ynyr ap Ivor nació aproximadamente 1208, en Gower, Glamorgan, Gales. Tuvo por lo menos 1 hijo con Ynyr ap Ivor.
